description:
You are acting as a Linux systems-engineer who has been asked to tighten the
    permissions on a small project that lives entirely under the normal,
    non-privileged account “user”.  No root or sudo access is available, so
    everything must be done inside the existing home directory tree.

    Goal
    ----
    1. Create a directory called  /home/user/secure_project
       with three immediate sub-directories:
           • bin
           • data
           • logs

    2. Apply the following octal permissions (symbolic equivalents are shown only
       for your convenience – the automated test will examine the octal values):

           • /home/user/secure_project           →  750  (u=rwx, g=rx,  o=―)
           • /home/user/secure_project/bin       →  750
           • /home/user/secure_project/data      →  700  (u=rwx, g=―,  o=―)
           • /home/user/secure_project/logs      →  750

    3. Inside /home/user/secure_project/logs create a text file called
       /home/user/secure_project/logs/access.log that contains exactly four
       lines, no blank lines and **trailing NEWLINE after the final line**.
       Each line must describe one directory and its permission in the precise
       format below (all lowercase, use the colon exactly once, no spaces
       before/after the colon):

           directory_name:octal

       The lines must appear in this exact order:

           secure_project:750
           bin:750
           data:700
           logs:750

    4. Set the permission of access.log itself to 640.

    Output verification hint
    ------------------------
    • The directory tree will be inspected with `stat -c %a <file>` to confirm
      octal permissions.
    • The content of access.log must match *byte-for-byte* (including the
      terminating newline on the fourth line) the four-line block specified
      above.
    • The automated tester will **fail** if permissions or ordering deviate, or
      if extra characters (spaces, carriage-returns, tabs) are present.

    What you need to do
    -------------------
    Perform whatever shell commands you think are necessary to reach the desired
    end-state described above.  You should see no error messages.  When you are
    confident everything is correct, you may list the tree or display the log
    file in the console so that a human can visually confirm the result, but
    remember that the automated test will rely only on the exact filesystem
    state outlined here.
truth:
The test harness will check the following final state:

    1. Directories exist:
       • /home/user/secure_project
       • /home/user/secure_project/bin
       • /home/user/secure_project/data
       • /home/user/secure_project/logs

    2. Octal permissions:
       • /home/user/secure_project               →  750
       • /home/user/secure_project/bin           →  750
       • /home/user/secure_project/data          →  700
       • /home/user/secure_project/logs          →  750
       • /home/user/secure_project/logs/access.log → 640

    3. File content (exact, including terminating newline after “logs:750”):

       secure_project:750\n
       bin:750\n
       data:700\n
       logs:750\n
name: task_000000_566d8584
